skill.name: treesitter-patterns
skill.description: Framework-neutral guidance for using tree-sitter parsers, queries, visitors, and language plugins in code analysis projects.
skill.summary: Build reliable tree-sitter parsing workflows with queries, visitors, error handling, and performance guidance.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 34 external command alerts and 6 weak cryptography blockers in SKILL.md. Manual review found false positives in Markdown prose, inline code terms, and fenced tree-sitter examples; no executable script, network access, credential handling, prompt injection, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Backticks Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution alerts occur in Markdown instructions, inline code, tables, and fenced examples. SKILL.md contains no runnable shell script, Ruby code, subprocess call, or command construction path at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation and parser examples, not executable skill code. No evidence found of shell execution or user-controlled command invocation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prose Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts map to ordinary Markdown metadata, headings, table text, and schema examples. No cryptographic API, cipher selection, hashing workflow, key material, or security-sensitive implementation appears in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain descriptive text rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, DES, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic implementation being used.

content.value_statement: Tree-sitter projects often need consistent parsing patterns across languages. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for parsers, queries, visitors, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to initialize tree-sitter parsers for language-specific analysis.
Shows common node navigation patterns for named and unnamed AST children.
Provides query examples for functions, classes, imports, and decorators.
Outlines visitor patterns for extracting structured information from syntax trees.
Covers parse error collection and performance guidance for large codebases.
limitations: It provides guidance only and does not include runnable parser packages.
Referenced cookbook files are not present in the scanned package.
Examples focus mainly on Python-style tree-sitter usage.
Project-specific grammar details still require checking each language grammar.

use_cases.title: Add AST Extraction to a Developer Tool
use_cases.description: Use the patterns to identify functions, classes, imports, and decorators from parsed source files.

use_cases.title: Design Cross-Language Query Rules
use_cases.description: Create consistent query strategies while accounting for different node names across supported languages.

use_cases.title: Improve Parser Reliability
use_cases.description: Add error handling, edge case checks, and performance practices before processing large repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Parser Setup Steps
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new code analysis feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the treesitter-patterns skill to outline the tree-sitter parser setup steps for my target language and analysis goal.

prompt_templates.title: Write Query Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate query planning for common code constructs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the treesitter-patterns skill to design tree-sitter queries for functions, classes, imports, and decorated declarations in this project.

prompt_templates.title: Review AST Visitor Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced extraction design for a custom analyzer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the treesitter-patterns skill to review my AST visitor approach and suggest improvements for traversal, error handling, and output structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Language Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture work across several grammars.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the treesitter-patterns skill to plan a multi-language tree-sitter analysis workflow with query organization, parser versioning, and performance safeguards.

output_examples.input: Ask for a plan to extract functions and imports from Python files.
output: A parser setup checklist for Python.
A query plan for function and import captures.
A validation step for syntax errors and missing nodes.

output_examples.input: Ask for guidance on a visitor that collects class and function names.
output: A visitor structure with generic traversal behavior.
Advice for using field names when available.
Warnings about parse errors and language-specific node names.

output_examples.input: Ask how to scale tree-sitter parsing across a repository.
output: A batching approach for multiple files.
Performance notes about selective queries and memory use.

best_practices: Test each query against real files from the target language.
Collect parse errors before trusting extracted AST data.
Pin parser versions so node names and grammar behavior stay stable.
anti_patterns: Do not assume every file parses without syntax errors.
Do not traverse entire syntax trees when a focused query is enough.
Do not reuse node names across languages without checking each grammar.

faq.question: Does this skill install tree-sitter parsers?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and examples, but parser packages must be installed separately.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: It is framework-neutral and discusses common patterns for Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and Rust node types.

faq.question: Can it generate project-specific queries?
faq.answer: It can guide query design, but final queries should be tested against the target grammar and source files.

faq.question: Does it include complete cookbook files?
faq.answer: No. The scanned package only includes SKILL.md, and the referenced cookbook files are not present.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found no executable malware behavior, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection.

faq.question: What should users verify before production use?
faq.answer: Users should verify parser versions, grammar node names, parse error handling, and performance on representative repositories.
